title: Affidavit for Uncontested Trial
description: Filed when you are applying for divorce or another family law order and the other party is not contesting (disputing) the matter. Allows the court to grant the order based on your written evidence without a live hearing. Must be sworn before a commissioner of oaths.

steps.title: About This Affidavit
steps.description: Form 26B allows a divorce to proceed without a court hearing. A judge reviews the written materials and grants the divorce if all requirements are met.

fields.label: When is Form 26B used?
fields.content:
**Form 26B is used when:**
- You have filed for divorce and the other party has NOT contested it (has not filed an Answer disputing the divorce)
- OR both parties agree to a joint divorce application
- There is no need for a court hearing — the judge decides on the written materials

**You will also need:**
- A certified copy of your marriage certificate
- Your Form 36 (Affidavit for Divorce) — already sworn
- Form 25A (Order for Divorce) — the draft order for the judge to sign
- Proof of service on the other party (Form 6B) if they were not a joint applicant

**After the divorce is granted:** The divorce order becomes effective 31 days after it is made, unless the judge waives the waiting period.

fields.label: Courthouse
options: St. Catharines — Superior Court of Justice
Thunder Bay — Superior Court of Justice

options.label: Sole application — I am the only applicant
Joint application — both parties are applicants

fields.label: You are swearing this affidavit as

options.label: The Applicant
One of the joint applicants

steps.title: The Marriage
steps.description: Confirm the details of your marriage. These must match your marriage certificate exactly.

fields.label: City or town where you were married
Province or country where you were married
Your full name at the time of marriage (if different from current name)
fields.placeholder: Leave blank if your name has not changed

fields.label: Other party's full name at the time of marriage (if different from current name)
fields.placeholder: Leave blank if their name has not changed

fields.label: Do you have a certified copy of your marriage certificate?

options.label: Yes — I will attach a certified copy
No — I need to obtain one
I have applied for one and it is pending

fields.helpText: A certified copy of your marriage certificate is required to be filed with your divorce application. Obtain one from ServiceOntario or the vital statistics office in the jurisdiction where you were married.

fields.label: If you do not have a marriage certificate, explain why and what steps you are taking
fields.placeholder: e.g. We were married in India. I have applied to the Indian government registry for a certified copy and expect to receive it within 8–10 weeks. I am asking the court to allow me to file the certificate once received.

steps.title: Residence & Jurisdiction
steps.description: The court must confirm it has jurisdiction to grant the divorce. At least one spouse must have lived in Ontario for at least one year before the divorce application was filed.

fields.label: Your current address
fields.placeholder: e.g. 123 Main St, Toronto ON M5V 1A1

fields.label: How long have you lived in Ontario?

options.label: More than 1 year — I have lived in Ontario for at least 1 year
Less than 1 year — I have not lived in Ontario for a full year yet

fields.helpText: You must have been ordinarily resident in Ontario for at least one year immediately before the divorce application was filed.

fields.label: Note on residency requirement
fields.content: If you have not lived in Ontario for at least one year, check whether your spouse has — if so, they may need to be the applicant, or you may need to wait until the one-year requirement is met.

fields.label: Do you know the other party's current address?

options.label: Yes
No — their address is not known to me

fields.label: Other party's current address
fields.placeholder: e.g. 456 Oak Ave, Ottawa ON K1A 0A1

steps.title: Separation
steps.description: Confirm the separation facts. One year of separation is the most common ground for divorce in Canada.

fields.label: Date you and your spouse separated
fields.helpText: The date one or both of you decided the marriage was over.

fields.label: Have you lived separate and apart for at least one year as of today?

options.label: Yes — we have been separated for at least one year
No — we have not yet been separated for one year

fields.helpText: You must have been separated for at least one year before the divorce can be granted.

fields.label: Separation requirement not yet met
fields.content: You can file a divorce application before the one-year anniversary of your separation — but the court will not grant the divorce until one year has passed. Make note of your one-year anniversary date and follow up with the court at that time.

fields.label: Did you and your spouse attempt to reconcile after separating?

options.label: Yes — we had a period of attempted reconciliation
No — we did not attempt to reconcile

fields.label: Describe the reconciliation attempt(s)
fields.placeholder: e.g. We reconciled from October 1 to October 28, 2024 (28 days). We then separated again on October 29, 2024. Our total reconciliation period was less than 90 days so our one-year separation clock was not reset.
fields.helpText: Reconciliation periods of 90 days or less (combined) do not reset the one-year clock. Periods over 90 days do reset it.

fields.label: Is there any reasonable chance of reconciliation?

options.label: No — there is no reasonable chance of reconciliation
Yes — there may be a possibility

fields.helpText: You must confirm there is no reasonable chance of reconciliation for the court to grant the divorce.

fields.label: Did you live in the same home after separating?

options.label: Yes — we continued to live in the same home after separating
No — we moved to separate homes when we separated

fields.label: Describe how you lived separately in the same home
fields.placeholder: e.g. After we separated on March 1, 2024, we continued to share the house due to financial constraints. We slept in separate bedrooms, had no intimate relationship, ate separately, and managed our finances independently. We told family and friends that we had separated. I moved out on September 15, 2024.
fields.helpText: The court must be satisfied you were truly living 'separate and apart' even if under the same roof. Be specific.

steps.title: Children
steps.description: Confirm whether there are children of the marriage and what support arrangements are in place.

fields.label: Are there any children of the marriage — under 18, or 18 or over but still dependent?

options.label: Yes
No — there are no children of the marriage

fields.helpText: Children of the marriage include biological, adopted, and step-children who were treated as children of the family.

options.label: The Applicant
The Respondent
Both parents (shared/equal time)
Another person

fields.label: Is this child still dependent (if 18 or over)?

options.label: Yes — still in school or otherwise dependent
No — fully independent
Under 18 — automatically a child of the marriage

fields.label: Are reasonable arrangements in place for child support?

options.label: Yes — child support is in place
No — child support has not been arranged

fields.helpText: The court will not grant the divorce unless satisfied that reasonable support arrangements exist for the children.

fields.label: Describe the child support arrangements
fields.placeholder: e.g. The Respondent pays child support of $1,200 per month pursuant to the Federal Child Support Guidelines, as set out in the separation agreement dated March 15, 2025. Payments are made through the Family Responsibility Office.

fields.label: Describe the parenting (custody/access) arrangement
fields.placeholder: e.g. The children reside primarily with the Applicant. The Respondent has parenting time every other weekend from Friday 6 p.m. to Sunday 6 p.m. and Wednesday evenings from 4 p.m. to 7 p.m. This is set out in the separation agreement dated March 15, 2025.

steps.title: Service on the Other Party
steps.description: Confirm how the other party was served with the divorce application, or why service is not required.

options.label: The other party was personally served with the application
The other party was served by mail
Service was accepted by the other party's lawyer
This is a joint application — service is not required
The court dispensed with service

fields.label: Date the other party was served
fields.helpText: This should match the date on your Form 6B (Affidavit of Service).

fields.label: Did the other party file an Answer contesting the divorce?

options.label: No — they did not file an Answer
Yes — they filed a response

fields.helpText: Form 26B can only be used if the divorce is uncontested. If the other party filed an Answer, you may need to proceed to a hearing instead.

fields.label: Other party filed a response
fields.content: If the other party filed an Answer to your divorce application, the divorce may be contested and cannot proceed on written materials alone. Contact the court clerk to discuss whether a hearing is required. Form 26B may not be the appropriate form for your situation.

steps.title: Previous Divorce Proceedings
steps.description: Disclose any previous divorce or family law proceedings between you and the other party.

fields.label: Are there any other divorce or family law proceedings between you and the other party in any court in Canada or elsewhere?

options.label: Yes
No

fields.label: Describe the other proceedings
fields.placeholder: e.g. There is an existing Ontario Superior Court of Justice proceeding (File No. FC-2024-12345) which is the proceeding within which this divorce application is filed. There are no other proceedings in any other court in Canada or internationally.

fields.label: Do you have a signed separation agreement?

options.label: Yes
No

fields.label: Describe the separation agreement
fields.placeholder: e.g. The parties executed a separation agreement dated March 15, 2025, which addresses parenting, child support, spousal support, and property division. A copy is filed with the court.

steps.title: Collusion & No Reconciliation
steps.description: Standard declarations required by the Divorce Act.

fields.label: Declaration regarding collusion
fields.checkboxLabel: I confirm that there has been no collusion in relation to this divorce application — meaning no agreement, conspiracy, or arrangement to fabricate or suppress evidence or to deceive the court.
fields.helpText: Collusion is extremely rare in practice but must be declared. It refers to a secret agreement to mislead the court — for example, fabricating grounds for divorce.

fields.label: Declaration regarding condonation
fields.checkboxLabel: I confirm that I have not condoned any conduct that might otherwise be raised as a ground for divorce.
fields.helpText: Condonation means forgiving a matrimonial offence (such as adultery or cruelty) and resuming the marriage knowing about it. For separation-based divorces, this is generally not applicable.

fields.label: Grounds for divorce

options.label: Separation — we have been separated for at least one year (most common)
Adultery — my spouse committed adultery
Physical or mental cruelty

fields.helpText: Virtually all Canadian divorces proceed on the grounds of one year's separation. Adultery and cruelty are very rarely used because they are more difficult to prove.

steps.title: Name Change (Optional)
steps.description: If you wish to resume a former name after the divorce, this can be included in the divorce order.

fields.label: Do you want to resume a former surname after the divorce?

options.label: Yes — I want to resume a former name
No — I do not want to change my name

fields.helpText: You can resume a surname you used before the marriage. The divorce order itself serves as proof of the name change — no separate process is required.

fields.label: Are you asking the court to waive the standard 31-day waiting period before the divorce takes effect?

options.label: No — the standard 31-day effective date is fine
Yes — I am asking the court to waive the 31-day period

fields.helpText: The divorce normally becomes effective 31 days after the order is made — this allows time for an appeal. The waiting period is rarely waived; it requires a compelling reason such as an imminent remarriage.

fields.label: Reason for requesting the 31-day waiver
fields.placeholder: e.g. Both parties consent to the divorce taking immediate effect. The Applicant plans to remarry on [date] and needs the divorce to be final before that date. Both parties waive their right to appeal.

steps.title: Documents Checklist & Declaration
steps.description: Confirm what documents you are filing with this affidavit and swear to the accuracy of your information.

fields.label: Documents you are filing with this affidavit
fields.helpText: Check all that apply. You must file all required documents together.

options.label: Divorce application (Form 8A)
Form 36 — Affidavit for Divorce (sworn)
Form 25A — Order for Divorce (draft, unsigned)
Certified copy of the marriage certificate
Form 6B — Affidavit of Service (proof of service on the other party)
Separation agreement (if one exists)
Minutes of settlement or draft consent order
Other court forms or documents

fields.label: Describe other documents being filed
fields.placeholder: e.g. Financial statement Form 13, property valuation report

options.label: Swear (on a religious text)
Affirm (non-religious solemn declaration)

fields.label: Municipality where you will swear or affirm this affidavit
Date of commissioning (leave blank to complete at courthouse)
Declaration
fields.text: I, the deponent, declare that the contents of this affidavit are true and complete to the best of my knowledge and belief. I make this solemn declaration conscientiously believing it to be true and knowing that it is of the same force and effect as if made under oath.
fields.checkboxLabel: I confirm this declaration is true and accurate.

pdfMapping.notes: Form 26B — Affidavit (Divorce) for uncontested divorce applications. Must be sworn before a commissioner of oaths. Filed with Form 36, Form 25A, marriage certificate, and proof of service. See ontario.ca/page/family-law-forms.
